htmlContent:
The moment you feel eyes on you, your body freezes

I know this feeling. You are at the grocery store and someone is watching you choose an avocado. Or you walk into a meeting and all heads turn. Or your partner just looks at you a certain way, and suddenly you can't move. Your throat tightens. Your breath gets shallow. Your limbs feel heavy. You might even feel like you are disappearing.

This is not weakness. This is not social anxiety you just need to push through. This is your nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do. It is trying to keep you alive.

Here's the thing. The freeze response is the oldest survival strategy in the mammalian brain. It predates fight and flight. When a predator is near, sometimes the safest thing is to become completely still. Motionless. Invisible. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, explained that our nervous system constantly scans for cues of safety and danger. When it detects a threat, it can shut down our mobilization systems entirely. You don't run. You don't fight. You just stop.

And being watched? That is a massive cue of potential danger. Because in our evolutionary history, being watched meant a predator was tracking you. It meant someone was judging whether you were prey. Your body doesn't know the difference between a predator and a colleague staring at you during a presentation. It just knows eyes are on you, and that has historically been very dangerous.

Think about it. When you were a child, did being watched feel safe? Or did it feel like scrutiny? Did eyes on you mean love and connection, or did they mean criticism, punishment, or performance pressure? For most of us with complex trauma, being watched was never neutral. It was a signal to brace for impact.

How the freeze response actually works in your body

Let's get specific about what happens when you freeze. Your dorsal vagal nerve, the oldest part of your parasympathetic nervous system, activates. This is the part we share with reptiles. It slows your heart rate. It drops your blood pressure. It can even make you feel dissociated, like you are watching yourself from outside your body.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, calls freeze the "deer in the headlights" response. But it is more than just a metaphor. Your muscles tense in a specific pattern. Your breath becomes shallow and high in your chest. Your gaze might go unfocused or fixed. You might feel a sense of collapse, like your bones are made of sand.

And here is where it gets tricky. The freeze response is designed to be temporary. In the wild, an animal freezes, the predator loses interest, and then the animal shakes off the immobility and goes back to normal. But for humans with trauma histories, freeze can become a chronic state. You get stuck there. Your nervous system keeps you partially immobilized because it never gets the signal that the threat has passed.

Being watched triggers this stuck freeze because it feels like a sustained threat. The person keeps looking. The situation doesn't resolve. Your body keeps waiting for the all-clear signal that never comes. So you stay frozen, sometimes for hours or even days after the event.

I have had clients describe this as feeling like a statue. Like they are trapped inside their own body. Like they can't speak or move or even blink. And then they feel shame about it, which makes the freeze worse. The shame tells them something is wrong with them. But nothing is wrong with them. Their nervous system is just trying to protect them from a threat that isn't actually there anymore.

If this resonates, you might want something to help your body feel safer during these moments. A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is the YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs. The weight can help signal safety to your nervous system, grounding you when the freeze starts to creep in.

The link between being watched and your trauma history

Not everyone freezes when watched. Some people get angry. Some people get anxious and fidgety. Some people perform. But if you freeze, it tells me something about your history.

It tells me that being seen was not safe for you. Maybe you were punished for expressing yourself. Maybe you were ignored until you did something wrong. Maybe you were sexualized or objectified. Maybe your caregivers used eye contact as a way to control or intimidate you.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score, has written extensively about how trauma lives in the body. He talks about how people with trauma histories often have a heightened startle response and a tendency to dissociate under stress. Being watched is a form of social stress. It triggers the same neural pathways that were activated during the original trauma.

Here is what I want you to understand. Your freeze response is not random. It is not a character flaw. It is a specific adaptation to a specific environment. Your body learned that being seen was dangerous, and it learned to go still as a way to survive. That kept you alive then. But now, it keeps you stuck.

And the wellness industry will tell you to just breathe through it. To meditate. To visualize a . But that is like telling someone with a broken leg to just walk it off. You cannot think your way out of a freeze response. You cannot positive-affirm your way out of a dorsal vagal shutdown. You have to work with the body, not against it.

One thing that can help is understanding the patterns you inherited. If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You by Mark Wolynn. It explores how trauma gets passed down through generations and how those patterns show up in your nervous system today.

What to do when you feel the freeze coming on

I am not going to tell you to just relax. That is insulting. But I can share some things that actually work, based on what I have learned from my own recovery and from the research.

First, orient. Look around the room. Notice the walls. The ceiling. The floor. Your nervous system needs to know where you are and that there is no immediate threat. Dan Siegel talks about how the prefrontal cortex can help regulate the amygdala. Orienting gives your higher brain a chance to come online.

Second, move slowly. The freeze wants you to stay still. So you have to trick your nervous system into thinking it is safe to move. Start with your fingers. Wiggle them. Then your toes. Then your shoulders. Tiny movements. No rush. Stay with me here. This can take several minutes. That is okay.

Third, use your breath. But not in a forced way. Just notice your exhale. Make it a little longer. The exhale is what activates the vagal brake. A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is the Breathing Exercise Tool. It can give your breath a physical anchor, which sometimes helps more than trying to control it with your mind.

Fourth, get curious. Instead of judging yourself for freezing, ask your body what it needs. This sounds weird, I know. But your freeze response is trying to communicate something. Maybe it needs to feel grounded. Maybe it needs to feel hidden. Maybe it needs to know that the person watching is not a threat. Janina Fisher, who integrates Internal Family Systems with trauma treatment, talks about how every part of us has a positive intention. Your freeze part is trying to protect you. Thank it. Then ask it what it needs to relax its grip.

And fifth, know that recovery is not linear. You will freeze again. That is not failure. That is your nervous system doing its job. The goal is not to never freeze. The goal is to freeze and come back faster. To recognize it sooner. To have more tools to work with.

If you want to understand more about how C-PTSD shapes your nervous system, One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving by Pete Walker. It offers a framework for understanding freeze and other trauma responses.

The problem with being told to just "show up"

I need to be direct about something. The modern wellness culture will tell you to show up as your . To be vulnerable. To let yourself be seen. And that is great advice for people with secure attachment. But for those of us with complex trauma, being seen is the most dangerous thing we can do.

We were punished for being seen. We were hurt for being vulnerable. Our authenticity got us in trouble. So when someone tells us to just be ourselves, our nervous system says, "Absolutely not. That is how we got hurt last time."

This is not resistance. This is wisdom. Your body remembers what your mind has forgotten.

So what do you do? You don't force yourself to be seen before you are ready. You don't push through the freeze. You work with it. You build safety slowly. You find people who can hold your freeze without judging it. You practice being seen in tiny doses. A moment of eye contact. A shared laugh. A vulnerable sentence that you can take back if you need to.

And you stop believing that there is something wrong with you for freezing. There isn't. You are not broken. You are not too sensitive. You are not weak. You are a person with a nervous system that learned to survive in a world that was not safe. And that nervous system is still trying to protect you, even when you don't need protection anymore.

Wild, right? The thing that kept you alive is now the thing that keeps you small. But it can change. It does change. I have seen it happen in my own body and in the bodies of the people I work with. The freeze can soften. The fear of being watched can fade. Not completely, maybe. But enough. Enough to let you live a bigger life.

And that is worth the work. Even when the work is slow. Even when the work looks like sitting still, wiggling your fingers, and waiting for your nervous system to trust that it is safe to come back into your body.
